result:
The rain started at 4:17 PM. I know this because I was staring at the clock on the microwave, willing the numbers to move faster, to fold into 5:00 like a magician’s trick. The microwave hummed its low, electric hum, a sound so constant you forget it’s there until it stops. The rain started with a single drop on the kitchen window—a fat, heavy thing that splattered against the glass like a signal. Then another. Then a dozen. Then the sky decided to stop hesitating and just let go.

I was supposed to be writing something important. A report, maybe. An email. A grocery list. Something with purpose, with a beginning and a middle and an end. But the rain made everything feel temporary, like the words I was about to type would just wash away if I let them sit too long. So I didn’t type. I just watched.

The street outside my window is unremarkable. It’s the kind of street you drive down without noticing, the kind where every house has a lawn and a driveway and maybe a tree that’s been there longer than anyone remembers. The rain turned the asphalt into a dark mirror, reflecting the grey sky and the red taillights of a car that was idling at the stop sign. The driver was probably checking their phone, or singing along to the radio, or thinking about something they wished they hadn’t said. That’s the thing about rain: it doesn’t care about your regrets. It just falls.

I thought about a woman I used to know. Her name was Elena, and she had a laugh that sounded like a song you almost remember. We met at a coffee shop two years ago. I was reading a book I didn’t really like but felt obligated to finish; she was drawing something in a small notebook, her pen moving in loops and zigzags that didn’t look like anything but felt like everything. She looked up and smiled, and I said something stupid—I don’t remember what—and she laughed that laugh, and I knew right then that I was in trouble.

We spent three months together. Three months of late-night talks and early-morning texts. Three months of learning the map of each other’s lives—her love for old movies, my inability to fold a fitted sheet. We went to a concert where the bass was so loud it felt like a heartbeat. We argued about whether pineapple belongs on pizza. (I think it does; she was wrong. I told her that. She laughed again, even when she was wrong. That was the thing about her—she never got angry, not really. She just moved on, like water finding its level.)

And then one day, she didn’t. She had a job offer in another city, a city with better weather and more opportunities and none of the memories we’d made. She told me over dinner, and I nodded like I understood, like I was a grown-up who could handle this. But I didn’t understand. I still don’t. She left on a Tuesday, and I spent the rest of that week listening to the rain, because apparently that’s what I do when I don’t know what else to do. I listen to the rain.

The rain is softer now. It’s settled into a rhythm, a steady pat-pat-pat against the window. I used to think rain was sad—the soundtrack for breakups and bad news and days when the sun forgets to show up. But I’m not so sure anymore. There’s something honest about it. The rain doesn’t pretend. It doesn’t try to be anything other than what it is. It falls, and it falls, and it falls, until the ground is soaked and the air smells like wet concrete and the world feels like it’s been wiped clean.

I opened the window a crack. Just enough to let the sound in, sharp and real. A gust of wind pushed through, carrying a few drops that landed on my arm. They were cold, and for a second, I was a kid again, sitting on the porch of my grandmother’s house, watching the rain turn the yard into a puddle-jumping paradise. Back then, rain meant freedom—a chance to splash and scream and get dirty. Back then, I didn’t know that rain could mean a world of other things. Longing. Loss. The quiet ache of a love that didn’t last.

My phone buzzed. A text from my sister: *Did u feed the cat?*

I forgot about the cat.

Her name is Mabel, and she is, without question, the ruler of this household. She’s a grey tabby with green eyes that judge everything I do, from the way I pour cereal to the volume of my music. She was staring at me from the hallway, her tail twitching like a metronome set to *impatient*. I think she knows I forgot. I think she knows everything.

“Sorry, Mabel,” I said, and her ears flicked. I got up, walked to the pantry, and filled her bowl with the crunchy food she pretends to hate but eats every time. She padded over, gave my ankle a quick rub that was neither forgiving nor unkind, and began eating. The sound of her crunching joined the rain, a small domestic symphony.

I sat back down at the kitchen table. The notebook I’d been ignoring was still open, still blank. The report, the email, the grocery list—they could wait. The rain wouldn’t last forever. It never does. But for now, it was here, and so was I, and so was Mabel, and so was a quiet kitchen and a blank page that suddenly didn’t feel so empty.

I picked up my pen and started writing. Not the important stuff. Just this. Just the rain, and the memory of Elena, and a cat who doesn’t care about my deadlines. Just a Tuesday evening in a small apartment, where the sky wept and the world slowed down, and I let it.

The rain stopped at 5:38 PM. I know this because I looked up from the page, and the window was quiet, and the streetlights were flickering on, one by one, like hesitant stars. Mabel had curled up on the couch, her paws tucked under her, dreaming of mice or tuna or whatever cats dream about.

I closed the notebook. The words were there now. Messy and real and not quite done, but there. And that was enough. That had to be enough.

Tomorrow the sun would come back, and I would send the email, and write the report, and buy the groceries. But tonight, I had the rain. Tonight, I had a blank page that I filled with something true. And somewhere out there, in whatever city she ended up in, I like to think Elena was watching the same rain, listening to the same quiet, maybe drawing a loop or a zigzag in her notebook. Maybe thinking of me.

Probably not. But maybe.

And that’s the thing about writing, about rain, about the simple, unremarkable moments. They don’t have to mean anything to anyone else. They just have to mean something to you.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
